content.problemStatement:
You are given a graph of NNN vertices - numbered from 111 to NNN and MMM edges between them. 

The ithi^{th}ith edge connects the AiA_iAi​ node to BiB_iBi​. The edges are directed.

You live at the vertex 111, output all the vertices that you can visit. You can travel using the given edges.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a two integers NNN and MMM, denoting the number of vertices and edges.
Next MMM lines describes the edges. i−thi-thi−th of these MMM lines contains two space-separated integers AiA_iAi​ and BiB_iBi​ denoting there exists a directed edge from AiA_iAi​ to BiB_iBi​.


Output Format
Output all the nodes you can visit from the node 1, in increasing order of their index.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 200000
Sum of M over all test cases does not exceed 200000
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with two integers N and M – the number of vertices and directed edges. Then M lines follow, each containing two integers A and B denoting a directed edge from A to B.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a single line all vertices reachable from vertex 1 (including 1 itself) in increasing order, separated by a single space.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Starting from vertex 1 we can reach 3, then 2, then 4. Vertices 1, 2, 3, 4 are reachable; vertex 5 is not.
First test case: 1→2→3, so 1 2 3 are reachable. Second test case: there is only an edge 2→3, vertex 1 cannot leave itself, so only 1 is reachable.
No edges exist, therefore only the starting vertex 1 can be visited.

solution.approach: The graph is directed. Starting from vertex 1 we have to find all vertices that can be reached by repeatedly following outgoing edges. This is a classic reachability problem that can be solved with a simple graph traversal – either Depth‑First Search (DFS) or Breadth‑First Search (BFS). We store the adjacency list, run an iterative DFS/BFS from node 1, mark visited vertices and finally output all visited vertices in increasing order (by scanning 1…N). The traversal visits each vertex at most once and each edge at most once, giving linear time.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N+M) per test case, O(total N + total M) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N+M) for the adjacency list and the visited array.
